Yield Curve Slope Signal
← Back to dashboard

Yield Curve Slope Signal

Yield curve slope signal: tracking recession risk and monetary stance via curve shape.

As of 2035-10-01

Gemini Summary

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given the T10Y2Y at 0.60pp and T10Y3M at 0.39pp, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Regime shift, not Data uncertainty. (1)
  • The signal is currently in a Flat/Neutral regime, having successfully exited the deep inversion of 2023–2024. (1)
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Improving; Threshold Proximity: Near; Revision Sensitivity: Moderate.

Methodology Applied:

  • The composite uses robust rolling z-scores to normalise constant-maturity spreads for historical comparability. (1)
  • A hysteresis rule (v1.1) requires a sustained confirmation window (k=2 months) and a ±0.50 exit band to prevent "uninversion" whipsaws. (1)
  • Entry into a "Steepening" regime requires the average composite z-score to exceed 0.75. (1)
  • Yield Curve Slope Signal (latest observation: 2026-02-28). (1)

Key Dynamics:

  • Current momentum is positive, with Z_T10Y2Y (0.59) and Z_T10Y3M (0.44) both indicating a shift away from restrictive levels. (1)
  • The Composite C (0.51) sits exactly at the exit band, suggesting the transition out of inversion is now technically confirmed under hysteresis rules. (1)
  • Conditional Invalidation: Two consecutive month-end composite readings falling below -0.50 would trigger a return to an "Inverted" classification. (1)
  • Signal stability is improving, as the 10Y3M spread—a primary proxy for recession risk—has moved into positive territory (0.39pp). (1)

Scenario Balance:

  • Base Case dominant: Consolidation in a Flat/Neutral regime as policy expectations and term premiums stabilise.
  • Upside risk: Rapid entry into a "Steepening" regime if long-end yields rise relative to the 2-year on growth optimism.
  • Downside risk: Re-inversion triggered by aggressive front-end policy tightening or a collapse in long-term growth expectations.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months), reflecting the 2–6 quarter lead structure for economic cycle inflection. (1)
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium; the composite construction reduces idiosyncratic noise from individual tenors. (1)

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs policy stance and growth expectations by proxying the term structure of interest rates. (1)
  • Economic mechanism: Directional steepening implies easing funding stress and rising compensation for term risk (term premium). (1)
  • Cycle position: Not determined; transition from inversion typically precedes late-cycle stress or recovery phases. (1)

Regime Context:

  • Persistent transition from Inverted (mid-2023) to Flat/Neutral (late 2024–present). (1)
  • Direction of change: Strengthening, as the composite approaches the 0.75 "Steepening" entry threshold. (1)

Model Limitations:

  • QE/QT distortions and institutional demand (e.g., RRP balances) can flatten curves without traditional macro catalysts. (1)
  • Constant-maturity yields are interpolation-based and subject to revisions at inflection points. (1)

Data & References:

  • Latest observation (2026-02-28): T10Y2Y 0.60, T10Y3M 0.39, Composite 0.51. (1)
  • Primary drivers: T10Y2Y (Z=0.59) and T10Y3M (Z=0.44). (1)
  • Supplemental data: FRED series T10Y2Y and T10Y3M. (1)
